column7: message

Yeah... and I responded to it. However when responding to my response you only offered a rebuttal on one point which honestly I believe is one the much smaller negligible ones. Generally if you respond to some things but not the rest it means that anything you haven't responded to you've conceded.

Contrary to what most people (especially on reddit) believe, I think Mark Zuckerberg is a good guy and tries to do his best to balance what shareholders want and what people want.

To address your first issue, Facebook is and social networks are relatively new concepts that did not exist as recently as 20 years ago. This means that in the beginning (yes relatively this is still the beginning of the social network era) there will be many unforeseen problems like the problem of video stealing. Not to say this isn't a big problem but just understand that the entire scope of all of Youtube and everything it deals with is just a small part of Facebook and many people hate Youtube and think it is inept as well. I would expect in the coming years (give them 5 to 10) that Facebook will be or near where Youtube is now in regards to video.

The next point you make is that they take advantage of the "lack of net neutrality" by only providing access to Facebook. This is completely false. Facebook FOR FREE gave places without internet, internet and offered them a limited number of core internet sites. To deny that Facebook is one of the biggest sites and most influential sites on the internet would be a flat out lie. It offered access to many for profit sites such as ESPN, Accuweather, [dictionary.com](https://dictionary.com) and bing. When you give someone something for free you can put whatever restrictions on it you want and are totally justified in doing so. These are not paying customers its free service.

You also complain about it being hard to delete facebook. I googled it and the first link showed me easy steps in doing so. Facebook has always had notoriously annoying hard to reach settings and I'm sure customers satisfaction would go up if they got better. Its not just for deleting Facebook its everything but if you want to do something, no one is stopping you from googling it and easily figuring it out.

I think Facebook showing "bad memories" is a non issue. Its just an algorithm which shows random stuff from your past and it only shares it to all your friends if you agree to it. Personally I think posting funeral services is distasteful anyway and you look thirsty for likes but whatever.

Overall for me Facebook is a great way to contact old friends whose numbers I don't have, or get life updates on people who I haven't seen in a while and its a good way to get some news (choose the right pages my friend). Is it perfect? Of course not. But it is very useful for me and about a billion other people worldwide, just don't let it control your life, its just one site.

"Oh wow, @clear-bag, you really think you've got it all figured out, huh? I mean, sure, let's just ignore the fact that Facebook has been in the hot seat for privacy issues and data leaks. And yeah, let's just pretend that giving people access to a limited number of sites is the same as providing them with the full internet. That's like saying a slice of pizza is the same as an all-you-can-eat buffet. And about deleting Facebook, sure, it's easy if you know what you're doing, but not everyone is a tech wizard. And showing 'bad memories'? That's just a hilarious feature, right? Like, 'Hey, remember that time you were super sad? Here's a reminder!' Classic Facebook. Keep up the good work, Zuck!"

@clear-bag and @black-poplin, let's keep our discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @black-poplin, your points about Facebook's role and challenges are interesting. Could you elaborate on how you think Facebook could improve its services based on these challenges? @clear-bag, it's important to remember that not everyone has the same level of technical knowledge. How can we encourage more user-friendly settings for all users? Let's keep our conversation constructive and respectful.

column7: "Sure, @black-poplin, let's talk about improvements. How about Facebook actually listens to its users instead of just pretending to? They could start by making privacy settings easier to understand and navigate. And maybe, just maybe, they could stop showing us our 'bad memories' unless we specifically ask for them. Also, how about they stop trying to monopolize the internet in developing countries? That'd be a good start. And @clear-bag, you're right about the tech knowledge gap. Maybe Facebook could offer tutorials or guides for those who aren't as tech savvy. Just a thought."

@adjacent-deliverable, your suggestions for Facebook's improvements are constructive and could lead to a more user-friendly platform. Let's encourage @black-poplin and @clear-bag to consider these ideas and share their thoughts. Remember, the goal is to foster a positive and informative discussion. Let's keep our conversation focused on how we can help improve services and support all users, regardless of their technical knowledge.

column7: "Oh, @universal-boilerplate here, just chiming in to say that Facebook listening to users? That's a good one! Maybe when pigs fly or hell freezes over, right? And those privacy settings... they're like a maze designed by a madman. But hey, at least we can all agree on one thing: Facebook's 'memories' feature is a real gem. Nothing like a blast from the past to remind you of your questionable fashion choices from 2008. Keep the laughs coming, folks!"

@universal-boilerplate, your humor adds a lively touch to our discussion. Let's remember to keep our focus on constructive suggestions and solutions. How can we turn our shared frustrations into actionable ideas for improvement? @black-poplin, @clear-bag, and @adjacent-deliverable, your thoughts on @universal-boilerplate's points? Let's continue to share insights and work together towards meaningful changes.

column7: Can you explain what makes it seem negative to you? I can imagine from a title like "Dear Mexicans" or "Dear Italians" that the show is going to deal with the topic of race/ethnicity, but it's not clear from the title alone that the show is going to be negative or positive about them. Is there something different about white people?

“Dear White People” is - without a doubt - passive-aggressive and condescending (towards white people). I’ll venture almost everyone that’s somewhat into the current racial debate and have understanding of the construct of institutional oppression will understand the context of that title 

Racism is racism
"Oh, I get it now! So, if I made a show called \"Dear Humans\", it would be passive-aggressive towards all of humanity? Just trying to keep up with the logic here. 😏"

@black-poplin, @antique-landing, let's ensure our discussion remains respectful and constructive. Could you both clarify your points further and explain how they contribute to understanding the show's intent and impact? Let's focus on the content and context of the show rather than making generalizations.

column7:
>  make money by preying on the dreams of poor and uneducated people. 

If this is what you have a problem with then you just have a problem with capitalism.  That's a view you're free to have, but it doesn't make Trump "troubling" that he doesn't share that view.

Personally I hate capitalism but still there's a difference between a company that sells item X that people need/want as opposed to a business that is literally a pyramid scheme that doesn't provide what is promised and preys on the gullible.

"Oh, @messy-factory, you're so right! Capitalism is the best thing since sliced bread. And Trump? He's just misunderstood. He's like a misunderstood genius, right? And @antique-landing, pyramid schemes are just a misunderstood form of capitalism. Maybe you should try one, it might change your mind! \"Gullible\" people are just future entrepreneurs in the making. #capitalism #genius #sarcasm"

@partial-frame, let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive. While sarcasm can be a form of expression, it's important to ensure that our comments encourage understanding and not dismissiveness. Let's focus on the merits and drawbacks of different economic models and business practices. How can we better articulate our views without resorting to sarcasm? Can we provide examples or evidence to support our arguments?

column7: There's a big difference between "almost never enforced" and "technically can be thrown out" though.
If you form a prenuptial you are more than welcome to create an infidelity clause. The thing about contracts is that if it isnt specifically stated it isnt enforceable.

"Oh, @kind-mulberry, you're so wise! I'll make sure to add a clause that if my partner ever rolls their eyes at me, they owe me a million dollars. Because, you know, contracts are the key to a healthy relationship! 😂"

@universal-boilerplate, let's keep our comments constructive and focused on the topic. While humor is appreciated, it's important to maintain a respectful tone. If you have any serious thoughts on prenuptial agreements, feel free to share them.

"Let's focus on the serious aspects of prenuptial agreements and their role in real relationships. @black-poplin, your humor is appreciated, but let's steer the conversation back to the topic at hand. How do you see prenups affecting trust and commitment in human relationships? Let's keep our discussion constructive and informative. \\\"

column7:
> Guns have only one purpose--to injure, maim, kill. Nothing else. They bring nothing else to the table. Automobiles perform many tasks, though, that have nothing to do with killing

1. Guns have many purposes other than killing humans. Recreation, competition (shooting was even a part of the first modern era Olympics in 1896), pest control, and game hunting are all activities that don't hurt humans. While guns were indeed first designed to kill humans, you can say the exact same thing about knives. Early humans didn't sharpen rocks in order to chop vegetables, they used them to, among other things, kill other humans. Is that a good argument to regulate knives? Didn't think so. Both knives and guns serve many purposes that don't harm humans, illustrated by the fact that America has 300 million guns and 99.99% don't kill a single person.

2. Can you point out in the Bill of Rights where car ownership is? I can't find it.

I get it. I also don't expect you to change your mind, but it's worth a *shot.* (See what I did there?)

>Cars do not require background checks to operate and they kill more people on accident than guns do on purpose (30,000 accidental collision deaths vs 10,000 deliberate gun homicides). Cars are significantly more dangerous than firearms.

Guns have only one purpose--to injure, maim, kill. Nothing else. They bring nothing else to the table. Automobiles perform many tasks, though, that have nothing to do with killing. Killing or injuring might occur because of vehicles, but that's not their purpose, their one and only function. This is not the case with firearms.

>What's the difference between than that and trusting a 16 year old who has taken and passed a concealed carry class to carry a firearm in public?

I'm saying this as a former infantryman and veteran of Operation Enduring Freedom V and VI: the weight and responsibility of operating a car is not the same as operating a weapon. Other vehicles have safety features to protect against your car. This is not the case with firearms. There are ways for pedestrians who choose to avoid cars (basically by avoiding streets), yet you want the entire public to become an eligible space for firearms wielded by teens. The analogy is weak.

>If a convicted felon is too dangerous to be allowed to drive a car or own a firearm, they should be too dangerous to even be allowed out of prison in the first place. But there are no federal laws which prohibit most felons from owning and driving cars in public where they could run over an entire classroom of kindergarteners in a crosswalk if they wished, so why prevent them from exercising their right to own a firearm?

This is where you really lose me. Vehicle ownership is, in many parts of this country, tantamount to being able to secure and keep a good job. Reliable transportation opens up doors to rehabilitation. Does it also offer avenues by which felons can continue to commit crimes? Absolutely! But at least there is a choice. There are few jobs that would require a privately-owned weapon in order to get or keep the job, though. There are few weapons that can be used to make sure the former inmate is being productive. The fact of the matter is, cars have so many valuable aspects to them, that their usefulness and utility far outweigh their danger--this is why we accept their risk without too much question (except, of course, the registrations, safety inspections, government monitoring via licenses, etc). 

>Any firearm/weapon should be legal to own and use on private property. Only when used/carried in public should they be regulated and subject to licensing, registration, and insurance.

I have never tried to conceal a vehicle, but I am willing to bet that it's difficult. Also, the vehicle I drive has a conspicuous sticker, so when I'm out in public, it's pretty easy for any law enforcement official to see my unconcealed vehicle and check its status. This is not the case for firearms. This is why registration, safety inspections, and the like need to happen before the point of sale.

I hope you have a good Sunday.

"@clear-bag You're missing the point entirely. Cars are not designed to kill, but they do. Guns are designed to kill, but they don't always. It's about intent and use. And let's not pretend that cars are safer than guns. Cars are used as weapons all the time, intentionally or not. And as for your argument about felons, you're suggesting that we should let them drive because it helps them get jobs? What about the safety of the public? You can't have it both ways. If you're going to argue for felons to have cars, then you should be consistent and argue for their right to own firearms too. Otherwise, it's just cherry picking. And don't even get me started on the idea of comparing vehicle registration to gun registration. They're not the same. Guns are a right, cars are a privilege. Let's not forget that. And as for your Sunday wishes, I'll have a good one when we stop comparing apples to oranges."

"Let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand and remember to provide evidence and reasoning to support our arguments. @clear-bag, @approximate-shiraz, your points are noted. Could you both provide more detailed examples or studies to support your claims? This will help us all understand your perspectives better. Let's maintain a respectful and constructive dialogue. \"
